pr.title: don't have to rerun frank on untouched files
pr.author: BR-

summary: A one-line Makefile fix made the Frank epilogue post-processing target update its output timestamp after running, preventing make from rerunning Frank on unchanged C files. The PR body demonstrates the practical effect: a no-op build previously reprocessed many files, while afterward it immediately reported the final DOL as OK.

key_files.reason: Added `touch $@` to the epilogue object rule so the generated/fixed output is considered up to date by make.

what_changed: In the Makefile rule `$(EPILOGUE_DIR)/%.o: %.c $(BUILD_DIR)/%.o`, after compiling with `$(CC_EPI)` and running `$(PYTHON) $(FRANK)`, the recipe now runs `$(QUIET) touch $@`.
The change ensures the epilogue object target's modification time is refreshed after Frank mutates or fixes the object output.

The PR body shows that before this change, a plain `make` with no source changes repeatedly printed many `Frank is fixing ...` lines for files such as `src/melee/lb/lblanguage.c`, `src/melee/ft/chara/ftpeach.c`, and many `src/sysdolphin/baselib/*.c` files.
After the change, the same no-op `make` output only reports `build/ssbm.us.1.2/main.dol: OK`, avoiding unnecessary relinking/conversion noise in the shown example.
smart_moves: Fixed the incremental build dependency symptom at the Makefile rule level instead of changing Frank itself.
Used `touch $@` after the post-processing step, which is the minimal signal make needs to consider the epilogue output current.
Kept the recipe under `$(QUIET)`, preserving existing Makefile output conventions.
The PR provided clear before/after command output, making the build-system regression and fix easy to verify.
decomp_lessons: When a decomp build pipeline has post-processing tools that rewrite object files, make's timestamp model must still be satisfied or no-op builds will repeatedly rerun expensive tooling.
For generated or transformed artifacts, ensure the final target timestamp reflects successful completion of every transformation step, not just the compiler invocation.
Small build-system fixes can materially improve decomp iteration speed by avoiding unnecessary reruns across broad source areas.
Before/after logs are especially useful for PRs that affect build behavior rather than code matching output.
naming_conventions: The Makefile uses uppercase variables for build tool paths and flags, including `EPILOGUE_DIR`, `BUILD_DIR`, `QUIET`, `CC_EPI`, `CFLAGS`, `PYTHON`, and `FRANK`.
The Makefile target pattern keeps epilogue-generated objects under `$(EPILOGUE_DIR)/%.o` while depending on both the source `%.c` and normal build object `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This PR did not change C source matching logic or assembly output directly.
The relevant matching-adjacent tactic is preserving reliable incremental behavior for Frank, the object post-processing/fixing tool used in the epilogue object path.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided PR context.
The PR was reviewed and merged quickly, but the dump does not include reviewer rationale beyond the merge.
searchable_terms: don't have to rerun frank on untouched files
follow_up_queries: Find other Makefile rules where generated or post-processed targets might not refresh `$@` timestamps.
Search for later PRs touching `FRANK`, `EPILOGUE_DIR`, or epilogue object build rules to see whether this pattern was reused or revised.
Check whether Frank mutates its input/output in a way that can leave timestamps older than prerequisites, explaining why `touch $@` was necessary.
